1: A pattern: “The heavens opened and I saw divine visions.” The opened heavens at Ezekiel’s commission anticipate the opened heavens at Jesus’s baptism (Matthew 3:16: “the heavens were opened”) and at Stephen’s martyrdom (Acts 7:56). When the eternal God addresses his prophets and his Son, the boundary between heaven and earth breaks open. Ezekiel’s vision is the OT prototype of the Christophanic openings: in both cases a figure emerges from heaven to commission a messenger for a resistant audience.
2: A structural note: The parenthetical date formula confirms the historical anchor of the vision — the fifth year of Jehoiachin’s exile (593 BCE). Ezekiel’s ministry occurs in the context of exile and displacement, the same conditions into which the Son of God was born (“no place to lay his head,” Luke 9:58) and in which the church ministers until the consummation.
3: A pattern: “The word of the LORD came to Ezekiel the priest.” Ezekiel is a priest who becomes a prophet — the combining of the priestly and prophetic offices in one person is the OT anticipation of the one Mediator who is both great High Priest and the Word of God (Hebrews 4:14; John 1:1). The hand of YHWH upon him (the prophetic commissioning formula) is the precursor to the Spirit’s descending on Jesus at his anointing (Luke 4:18).
4: A pattern: The theophanic storm from the north — whirlwind, cloud, fire, and gleaming metal — is the OT vocabulary for divine self-revelation in awesome power. This storm theophany pattern (found also in Job 38:1 and Psalms 50:3) points forward to Christ’s parousia described in the same register: lightning from east to west (Matthew 24:27), angels with great power (Matthew 24:31), the Son of Man coming in the clouds (Mark 13:26). The storm is how God appears when he comes to judge and redeem the earth.
5: A type: “From the center of the fire came what looked like four living creatures.” The four creatures that emerge from the divine fire reappear in Revelation 4:6-8 surrounding the throne of God and the Lamb. John of Patmos identifies them as the same creatures Ezekiel saw, and their ceaseless worship (“Holy, holy, holy, Lord God Almighty”) is now addressed to the enthroned Christ. The creatures that bore YHWH’s throne in Ezekiel’s vision bear the throne in the new creation — connecting the divine presence in both testaments.
6: A structural type: The four faces of each creature (human, lion, ox, eagle) are the fourfold symbol of created life at its highest expression. The church fathers, particularly Irenaeus, identified these four faces with the four Gospels: Matthew (human, the Son of Man’s genealogy), Mark (lion, opening roar of John the Baptist in the wilderness), Luke (ox, the sacrificial opening with Zechariah’s priestly ministry), John (eagle, soaring theological vision). Whether or not this precise correspondence holds, the fourfold witness to Christ is embedded in the structure of the divine throne-vision.
7: A structural type: The straight legs and bronze hooves of the creatures suggest purposeful, irresistible movement — they travel wherever the Spirit directs without obstruction. The risen Christ’s feet are described as “like refined bronze” in Revelation 1:15 — the same material imagery Ezekiel uses. The bodily posture of the creatures suggests that divine purposes move forward with invincible precision; the same irresistibility characterizes the advance of Christ’s kingdom.
8: A structural type: Human hands under the wings of the creatures represent the combination of transcendence (wings for heavenly movement) and agency (hands for earthly action). The Son of God assumes this combination in the incarnation: in Christ, transcendent divine power acts through human hands that heal, touch, and are pierced.
9: A structural note: The creatures move straight ahead without turning — wherever the Spirit directs, they go. This total responsiveness to the Spirit’s direction is the prototype of Christ’s own Spirit-directed ministry: he went precisely where the Father sent him, did nothing of his own initiative (John 5:19), and set his face like flint toward Jerusalem (Luke 9:51; Isaiah 50:7).
10: A type: The four faces (human, lion, ox, eagle) represent the fullness of created being in the presence of the Creator. In Christ, the fullness of divinity dwells bodily (Colossians 2:9) — he is the one in whom all four faces are unified: fully human, kingly like the lion (Revelation 5:5), sacrificial like the ox (Hebrews 9:12), and soaring in divine omniscience like the eagle (John 1:48). The four-faced creatures anticipate the one who recapitulates all creation in himself.
11: A structural type: The wings spread outward and touching neighbors, with two covering the body — the double function of wings (active flight and protective covering) is reflected in Christ’s gathering of his people: “how often I wanted to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings” (Matthew 23:37). The creatures’ covering wings are the OT type of the protecting, gathering wings of Christ.
12: A pattern: “Each moved straight ahead — wherever the spirit directed them to go, they went.” The absolute alignment between the Spirit’s will and the creatures’ movement models the trinitarian coordination in redemption. Jesus declares: “I can do nothing on my own... I seek not my own will but the will of him who sent me” (John 5:30). The merkabah creatures model the Spirit-directed obedience that Christ himself embodies perfectly.
13: A type: Fire moving back and forth among the creatures, blazing like coals, with lightning flashing — this is the fire of the divine presence that never goes out. The same fire that purged Isaiah’s lips (Isaiah 6:6-7) and descended at Pentecost (Acts 2:3-4) is the fire of divine holiness. Christ baptizes with the Holy Spirit and fire (Matthew 3:11) — he is the one who distributes the divine fire throughout the world’s communities.
14: A structural note: The creatures moved with the speed of lightning — instantaneous, incalculable speed of movement. The Son of Man coming at the last day is similarly described in terms of the instantaneous (Matthew 24:27: “as lightning flashes from the east to the west, so will be the coming of the Son of Man”). The creatures’ lightning speed is the prototype of the eschatological event that cannot be anticipated by ordinary time.
15: A type: The four wheels (ofannim) beside each creature — one wheel per creature — integrate earthly mobility with heavenly direction. The wheel is associated in Ezekiel with the providential movement of YHWH’s purposes through earthly history. In Christ, the eternal intersects with the historical: the Word became flesh (John 1:14) — the eternal wheel of divine purpose touched the earth at Bethlehem and rolled through history to the cross and resurrection.
16: A structural note: The wheels gleaming like beryl (topaz) with a wheel intersecting a wheel — the double-wheel structure allows movement in any direction without turning. The divine purposes in Christ cannot be trapped in a single historical trajectory: they move in every direction simultaneously (Ephesians 3:10: the manifold wisdom of God made known through the church) from the cross as its center point.
17: A structural note: The wheels could go in any of the four directions without turning — omnidirectional movement. The advance of the gospel into all nations and cultures without becoming “foreign” to any of them reflects this omnidirectionality: Christ is accessible from every direction (Revelation 5:9: “you were slain and purchased people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation”) without requiring any group to turn away from their particular history.
18: A type: “Their rims were high and awe-inspiring, and the rims of all four were covered with eyes all around.” The eyes covering the wheel-rims are the omniscience of the divine chariot — it sees in every direction, nothing escapes its perception. Christ is described in Revelation 1:14 with “eyes like a flame of fire” and in Revelation 5:6 the Lamb has “seven eyes, which are the seven spirits of God sent out into all the earth” — omniscient awareness that penetrates the cosmos. The wheels’ eyes are the OT type of Christ’s all-seeing, all-knowing presence.
19: A structural note: The synchronized movement of creatures and wheels — the creatures move, the wheels move; the creatures rise, the wheels rise — represents the perfect integration of heavenly direction and earthly execution. Christ’s ministry exhibits the same integration: every movement of the Son is the Father’s movement in him (“the Father who dwells in me does his works,” John 14:10).
20: A structural note: “For the spirit of the living creature was in the wheels.” The Spirit’s indwelling of the wheels explains their synchrony with the creatures. The same principle governs the Spirit’s work in Christ’s body (the church): the Spirit in the community enables the body to move in alignment with the head (Ephesians 4:4-16). The merkabah’s creature-wheel synchrony is the OT image of Spirit-body coherence.
21: A structural note: The creatures and wheels stop and move together in perfect coordination. The stillness of the cross — the moment when Christ was apparently stopped, immobilized on the cross — was precisely the moment of the Spirit’s deepest work, achieving what no movement could: the atonement. The stopping of the divine chariot at Golgotha was its most decisive act.
22: A type: “Spread out above the heads of the creatures was the likeness of a firmament, gleaming like crystal, awesome in its appearance.” The crystal firmament separating the creatures from the throne above them anticipates the torn veil of the temple (Mark 15:38) — the boundary between the creaturely and the holy was impenetrable until Christ opened it. At the ascension, the risen Christ passes through this boundary permanently as the forerunner (Hebrews 6:20), establishing permanent access through himself for all who are in him.
23: A structural note: The creatures’ wings under the firmament — spread straight, touching — create a vast canopy of creaturely worship below the divine throne. This posture of spread wings in reverence before the divine presence is the posture of all creation before the Creator. In Christ, the creator entered below the firmament, into creaturely space, so that creatures might ascend into divine space through him.
24: A type: “The sound of their wings like the roar of many waters, like the voice of the Almighty, a great rumbling like the noise of an army.” The sound of many waters is the signature of the divine voice in both Ezekiel and Revelation. Christ’s voice in Revelation 1:15 is “like the sound of many waters” — the same sound Ezekiel hears from the creatures’ wings is the voice of the exalted Christ. The sound of the divine approach that overwhelmed Ezekiel is the sound of the risen Christ speaking to his church.
25: A structural note: “A voice came from above the firmament over their heads. When they stood still, they lowered their wings.” The creatures’ stillness and wing-lowering at the sound of the divine voice is the posture of all creation when Christ speaks. At Gethsemane, the Roman soldiers fell to the ground at Jesus’s “I am he” (John 18:6) — a human echo of the creatures’ prostration before the divine voice.
26: A direct revelation: “Above the likeness of the throne was a form with a human appearance.” The human figure on the divine throne is the pre-incarnate Christ — YHWH in human form above the sapphire throne. Daniel sees the same figure: the Ancient of Days with the Son of Man approaching (Daniel 7:9-14). The revelation is climactic: the God who appears on the throne has a human form, anticipating the incarnation where the one who has always had human-form on the throne becomes human in history. The throne’s human-shaped occupant is identified in Revelation as the risen Jesus (Revelation 4:2-3; Ezekiel 22:1, 3).
27: A direct revelation: “From what appeared to be his waist upward I saw something like gleaming metal — fire enclosed all around.” The fiery, radiant figure of YHWH on the throne is described in language that Revelation applies directly to the risen Christ: “his face was like the sun shining in full strength” (Revelation 1:16); “his feet were like refined bronze, refined in a furnace” (Revelation 1:15). John’s inaugural vision of the exalted Christ in Revelation 1:13-16 is a deliberate reworking of Ezekiel’s throne-vision, identifying the risen Jesus as the fiery, radiant figure Ezekiel saw above the chariot-throne.
28: A direct revelation: “Like the rainbow that appears in the clouds on a rainy day, so was the radiance that surrounded him. Such was the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD.” The rainbow around the divine glory connects the Noahic covenant promise (Genesis 9:13-17: the rainbow as the sign that YHWH will never again destroy the earth by flood) with the divine presence. In Revelation 4:3, the rainbow “like an emerald” surrounds the throne — indicating that the covenant faithfulness symbolized in the rainbow is the permanent attribute of the one on the throne. Ezekiel falls prostrate before the glory; in John 1:14, this glory dwells in Jesus Christ and the disciples “beheld his glory.”
